coredata.dc:title: Silanized carbon quantum dots assembled porous glass and its luminescent properties
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Guangqi
Xu, Hong
Wang, Ziyi
Xie, Gening
Lv, Yang
He, Yingji
Zhang, Haoran
Zhang, Xuejue
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the silanized carbon quantum dots (Si CQDs) were assembled into nano-porous glass (NPG) by solution ultrasonic method. The morphology, structure, elementary composition, luminescence properties, water-resistance and anti-ultraviolet properties were systematically studied. Concentration quenching occurs when the Si CQDs doping content exceeds 7.73 mg/g. It is found that nano-porous glass matrix can effectively disperse and protect the Si CQDs, which realizes the luminescence of Si CQDs in solid state and enhances the fluorescence emission intensity of Si CQDs. In addition, the spatial confinement effect of nano-channels in Si CQDs/NPG composites makes it obtain excellent water-resistance. At the same time, the composites show better photo-stability under continuous UV irradiation. The above results provide a basis for its application in agricultural light conversion films.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silanized carbon quantum dots
Nano-porous glass
Luminescence properties
Photo-stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (a) NPG, (b) Si CQDs and (c) Si CQDs/NPG.
N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms and the corresponding BJH pore size distribution curve of the NPG.
XPS survey (BG. is background) and separation peaks of C 1s, and Si 2p of (a–c) Si CQDs and (d–f) Si CQDs/NPG.
(a) Normalized PL excitation and emission spectra of Si CQDs and Si CQDs/NPG. (b) Emission.
(a) Water-resistance of the Si CQDs/NPG composites, (b) Variation of PL intensity of Si CQDs/NPG composite and Si CQDs solution under UV irradiation.

head.ce:title: Silanized carbon quantum dots assembled porous glass and its luminescent properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKey Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the silanized carbon quantum dots (Si CQDs) were assembled into nano-porous glass (NPG) by solution ultrasonic method. The morphology, structure, elementary composition, luminescence properties, water-resistance and anti-ultraviolet properties were systematically studied. Concentration quenching occurs when the Si CQDs doping content exceeds 7.73 mg/g. It is found that nano-porous glass matrix can effectively disperse and protect the Si CQDs, which realizes the luminescence of Si CQDs in solid state and enhances the fluorescence emission intensity of Si CQDs. In addition, the spatial confinement effect of nano-channels in Si CQDs/NPG composites makes it obtain excellent water-resistance. At the same time, the composites show better photo-stability under continuous UV irradiation. The above results provide a basis for its application in agricultural light conversion films.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a clean and renewable energy, solar energy is always the focus of current agricultural science and technology research. The photosynthesis of plant chloroplasts is highly selective to light energy in the visible range. It mainly absorbs the red and blue light of the sunlight, but cannot make good use of other light [1]. Ultraviolet (UV) and near-ultraviolet (NUV) light may even cause oxidative damage to plants and hinder the growth of plants [2,3]. The previous researches have shown that increasing the content of blue light in the spectrum can effectively decrease the sensitivity of plants to UV light and thus reduce the damage of plants to UV light [4]. Therefore, through the selective absorption and energy conversion characteristics of the sunlight, the light conversion device converts the UV light from the sunlight into blue and red light, providing a better light environment for crops to improve yield and quality [5].

Fluorescent Carbon Dots (CDs) is one of the most popular materials among all kinds of light-conversion materials in plant application because of its high quantum efficiency, good chemical stability, excellent water solubility, low toxicity and eco-friendly nature [6–9]. At present, Li et al. reported the operation of CDs in mung bean plants in 2016 [10], and reported the light supplement effect of CDs on photosynthesis of lettuce in the following year [11]. Compared with CDs, silicon quantum dots (Si CQDs) have more extensive sources of raw materials, more stable optical properties and a wider spectral absorption range. Si CQDs have both the performance of CDs and the advantages of semiconductor quantum dots. At the same time, as an environment-friendly material, its unique combination of optical properties and biocompatibility makes it widely used in bio-imaging [12]. And silicon can also alleviate the stress on plant growth in adversity, such as the reduction of photosynthetic capacity caused by wheat and rice diseases, and the inhibition of crop photosynthesis due to drought [13,14]. However, efficient CDs-based composites are difficult to achieve, which could be understood in terms of the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching of aggregated CDs. Selecting suitable matrices to is the key to realize the solid state luminescence of CDs [15]. Nano-porous glass (NPG), as a kind of inorganic porous material, has many advantages such as good chemical stability, high mechanical strength, good processing performance, good heat resistance, three-dimensional spatial connectivity of pore channels, adjustable pore size and high surface activity [16,17]. It is a suitable matrices to ensure the CDs are monodispersed and efficient luminescence in the solid state.

Based on the above considerations, in this work, Si CQDs with good dispersion and uniform nanometer size were prepared by hydrothermal method and assembled into NPG. The morphology, structure, elementary composition, luminescent properties, water-resistance, UV resistance and luminescence stability of the Si CQDs/NPG were investigated in detail, which provides a foundation for its application in agricultural light-conversion materials.

Si CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Firstly, 11.16 g trisodium citrate was dissolved in 240 mL deionized water and bubbled with nitrogen to remove oxygen. Then 60 mL DAMO(DAMO; 95 %, AR; C8H22N2Si) was add into the above solution in a magnetic stirrer, which was reacted for 40 min with nitrogen protection. After this, the resultant precursor solution was transferred to an autoclave lined with Teflon-lined and heated at 200 ℃ for 12 h. Finally, after cooling to room temperature, Si CQDs were obtained by dialysis (1 kDa) to remove the residual reagent [18].

5 mL of the above solution was freeze-dried to obtain yellow Si CQDs powder, which was weighed by electronic balance. Based on the above data, the content of the prepared Si CQDs solution was calculated as 5.52 mg/mL.

According to the raw material formula of parent glass 7Na2O-27.5B2O3-64.5SiO2-1CaO (mol%), Na2CO3, B2O3, SiO2 and CaCO3 were mixed and ground in agate mortar, then the mixture was heated at 1500 ℃ for 0.5 h in a corundum crucible. Then the glass melt was poured into the preheated carbon mould and cooling to room temperature. After this, the glass was milled into a glass powder, sift to make sure the size less than 500 mesh and then put it into a clean alumina crucible for heat treatment and phase separation at 590 ℃ for 24 h. The heat-treated glass powder was put into 2 mol/L NaOH solution for ultrasonic treatment for 30 min to remove the residual silicon dioxide crystal layer on the particle surface due to the volatilization of sodium boron phase, and then cleaned to neutral with distilled water. After this, the glass powder was immersed in a mixture of 1 mol/L HCl and 1 mol/L NH4Cl (glass powder : solution = 1 g : 100 mL) at 95 ℃ for 24 h, and then immersed in hot water at 95 ℃ for 24 h. After washing the glass powder to neutral with deionized water, the glass powder was put into the drying oven at 80 ℃ for 4 h, and then store it in a clean container for standby [19–21].

Firstly, 0.5 g porous glass was placed in 20 mL absolute ethanol. Then Si CQDs x mL(x = 0.1–0.8) were add into the above absolute ethanol under vigorous stirring. The stirring was continued for about 10 min and then sonicated for 30 min. Finally, the samples were cleaned with absolute ethanol and dried to obtain the xSi CQDs/NPG.

Photoluminescence Excitation (PLE) and Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were analyzed by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a monochromator (resolution: 0.2 nm) and a 150 W Xenon lamp as the excitation source. The particle morphologies were obtained by a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, XL-30; Philips, North Billerica, MA) and a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, TECNAI12, Holland). The element types and contents were obtained by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific Escalab 250 Xi). The pore sizes were calculated by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method. All the optical properties of Si CQDs were measured by solution (5.52 mg/mL).

Fig. 1(a) shows a SEM image that the porous glass powder is irregular block structure and uneven particle size, with an average size of approximately 2–10 μm. The surface of the powder is smooth, there are many voids between the blocks, and the dispersibility is good. The porous glass powder has three-dimensional pore framework, and the pores are evenly dispersed and connected with each other as shown in Fig. 1(b). The average pore size is about 20 nm in the perspective.

Fig. 2(a) shows the TEM image of NPG without Si CQDs, which can be clearly seen the chaotic structure of nanopores like ant nest. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the shape of the Si CQDs is spherical with uniform size distribution, and its average particle size is about 4 nm according to the figure inset of Fig. 2(b) that is similar to the reported CQDs [22]. In the TEM image of Si CQDs/NPG, the Si CQDs are widely distributed in the middle of porous network as shown in Fig. 2(c). Thanks to this structure, the porous glass matrix well protects the internal Si CQDs from contact with the external environment (water and oxygen), and the dispersion effect greatly inhibits the decrease of luminescence intensity caused by the aggregation of Si CQDs, which can obtain stronger blue emission.

To further study the specific surface area and pore size distribution of NPG and to quantify the mesoporous characteristic parameters, a nitrogen adsorption-desorption test was performed on the NPG. As shown in Fig. 3 , a nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of NPG presented an H1-type isotherm with a hysteresis loop, which can generally indicate that the NPG are mesoporous [23]. In addition, the H1-type return loop, in most instances, indicates that the material had a cylindrical channel structure and uniform porosity distribution [24]. The pore size is mainly in the range of 2–50 nm, which further proves the existence of mesoporous structure. After calculation, the specific surface area of the NPG is 195.53 m²/g, the total pore volume is 0.30 cm³/g, and the pore size is 6.10 nm.

As show in Fig. 4(a) that the surface of Si CQDs is composed of Si, C, N and O. In Fig. 4(b), C 1s can be divided into two peaks, the peak at 284.8 eV is attributed to the C−C/C=C bond, and the peak at 287.4 eV is attributed to the C=O bond [25]. The two peaks of Si 2p at 101.9 eV and 102.6 eV are attributed to the Si−O bonds (Fig. 4(c)) [26–28]. As shown in Fig. 4(d) that the surface of Si CQDs/NPG are composed of Si, C and O. There is no other impurity element peak, which indicates that the purity of the composite is good. In Fig. 4(e), C 1s is divided into three peaks, the peak at 284.7 eV is attributed to the C−C/C=C bond, the peak at 286.1 eV is attributed to the C−OH bond and the peak at 287.4 eV is attributed to the C=O bond [29,30]. The two peaks of Si 2p at 103.2 eV and 103.6 eV are attributed to the Si−O bonds as shown in Fig. 4(f). The appearance of C−OH and indicates that Si CQDs and NPG are assembled successfully.

Fig. 5(a) and (b) show the fluorescence spectra of Si CQDs and Si CQDs/NPG. They have the same excitation spectra, indicating that the luminescence centers of the two are consistent. The difference in emission spectrum of 20 nm is due to the secondary absorption effect of quantum dots. The concentration of quantum dots distributed in the matrix is very low, which effectively attenuates this effect, so the bluer the spectrum shifts. Compared with Si CQDs, the best emission intensity of the composite is significantly improved, which further verifies that the porous glass matrix can protect the contact between the internal Si CQDs and the external environment (water and oxygen). At the same time, the dispersion effect greatly inhibits the decrease of luminescence intensity caused by the aggregation of Si CQDs, which can obtain stronger blue emission. As is given in Fig. 5(c), the luminescent intensity is very sensitive to the concentration of Si CQDs, determining the optimal Si CQDs doping content is 7.73 mg/g (0.7 mL Si CQDs solution). As the content of Si CQDs increases, the fluorescence emission intensity of Si CQDs/NPG first increased and then decreased. spectra of Si CQDs and Si CQDs/NPG. (c) PL spectra of xSi CQDs/NPG(x = 0.1 mL - 0.8 mL). (d) Time-resolved luminescence decay curves collected at 370 nm excitation for Si CQDs/NPG composites.

Fig. 5(d) shows the time-resolved decay curves of Si CQDs/NPG. At an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, the luminescence at 430 nm was monitored. The results show that all the attenuation curves are in line with the second-order exponential decay. According to Eq. (1) [31], the average fluorescence lifetime of the above eight samples are 4.80 ns, 6.37 ns, 7.46 ns, 7.52 ns, 7.56 ns, 8.36 ns, 9.62 ns, 9.28 ns, respectively.
(1)
τ
a v e
=
(
A 1
t 1 2
+
A 2
t 2 2
)
/
(
A 1
t 1
+
A 2
t 2
)

As the content of Si CQDs increases, the fluorescence lifetime of Si CQDs/NPG first increases and then decreases. When the Si CQDs content is 7.73 mg/g, the average life of the composite is the longest. This phenomenon is mainly due to the inner filter effect of Si CQDs in porous glass channels [32,33].

Under the protection of nano-porous glass, Si CQDs/NPG can maintain stable blue light emission under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp excitation in humid environment or even completely immersed in water. In Fig. 6(a) , the peak fluorescence emission intensity of Si QDs/NPG composites was normalized with immersion time, and the fitted straight line was basically horizontal. The results show that Si CQDs/NPG can maintain excellent luminescent stability in water. After immersion in water for 24 h, the luminescence effect of Si CQDs/NPG composite under 365 nm UV lamp excitation is still similar to that of the sample just immersed in water.

The excellent water-resistance and moisture-resistance of Si CQDs/NPG composites are mainly attributed to the NPG matrix. The spatial confinement effect of nano-channels in Si CQDs/NPG composites greatly inhibits the entry of water and protect the internal Si CQDs, which can improve the stability of internal carbon dots in the environment. Therefore, the Si CQDs/NPG has great potential in the application of agricultural light conversion agents, which can still keep good luminous effect in humid and rainy climate.

In order to verify the anti-ultraviolet properties and luminescence stability of Si CQDs /NPG, a comparative test was conducted between the 7Si CQDs /NPG and the Si CQDs solution prepared by Si CQDs solution. A 20 W UV lamp was used to irradiate the two samples for a long time in the same environment. As shown in Fig. 6(b), UV light has a certain degree of damage to the two samples. For Si CQDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of Si CQDs solution decreases to 55 % of the original in the first 10 min. After 20 min, its fluorescence intensity drops to 31 % of its original value. After 50 min of irradiation, the fluorescence of Si CQDs solution is almost quenched. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of Si CQDs/NPG decreased by less than 20 % in the first five minutes. After that, the decay rate of fluorescence intensity gradually decreased. Finally, after 50 min of irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of Si CQDs/NPG can be maintained at more than 35 %. This shows that the Si CQDs/NPG has good anti-ultraviolet properties, which can make the prepared agricultural light-conversion agent have longer service life.

In summary, we successfully assembled Si CQDs with porous glass by solution ultrasonic method for the first time, and the Si CQDs/NPG composites were obtained. With the SEM, TEM, XPS, BET, PLE and PL measurements, the microstructure, morphology, elementary composition, luminescence properties, and the stability of composite is systematically studied. Compared with Si CQDs, the best emission intensity of composite is significantly improved, which further verifies that the porous glass matrix can protect the contact between the internal Si CQDs and the external environment (water and oxygen). At the same time, the dispersion effect greatly inhibits the decrease of luminescence intensity caused by the aggregation of Si CQDs, which can obtain stronger blue emission. We determined the optimal Si CQDs doping content is 7.73 mg/g. In addition, the Si CQDs/NPG shows good water-resistance that the luminescence effect of Si CQDs/NPG composite which has immersed in water for 24 h is still similar to that of the sample just immersed in water. Moreover, the comparative test verifies the anti-ultraviolet properties and luminescence stability of Si CQDs/NPG. These results demonstrate that the composite has good fluorescence effect, which provides a basis for its application in agricultural light-conversion films.

Guangqi Hu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hong Xu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ziyi Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Gening Xie: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization. Yang Lv: Data curation, Methodology, Software, Validation. Yingji He: Investigation, Software, Visualization. Haoran Zhang: Formal analysis, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xuejue Zhang: . Bingfu Lei: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported from the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2023KJ122), the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI, and the National Undergraduate Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (No. 201910564035).
